EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lingering front over the area produced early morning showers and thunderstorms across the area. One of the storms that moved ashore produced some gusty winds leading to some minor damage near Hudson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Part of a tree fell on a fence in the Shadow Ridge subdivision. Winds were estimated in the 40-45 mph range based off of nearby home weather station reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of T.S. Erika crossed the forecast area from the Gulf region through the day. High moisture combined with forcing and weak onshore convergence around the remnant low brought waves of heavy rainfall and isolated strong storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flagler EOC measured 1.55 of rainfall in 30 minutes. A storm total of 1.57 was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong onshore flow backed water into the St. Johns basin. Tide water from Hopkins Creek was in the road at Kings Road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tide water from Hopkins Creek was over the roadway at Kings Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pre-frontal line of showers with embedded thunderstorms impacted the area during the afternoon hours. A few strong storms produced strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind damage occurred at the Hamilton County Elementary School. Multiple power lines were blown down and one was blown onto a roof. The roof was blown off of one of the school building. No injuries were reported. The school was evacuated. The cost of damage was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a deep convective cloud line over the Lower Florida Keys and an intersecting outflow boundary passing southwest from the nearshore Gulf of Mexico waters produced a small area of enhanced rainfall and widespread street flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports were received of widespread street flooding in the New Town section of northeast Key West.  Rainfall of 4.73 inches in 90 minutes was measured by a NWS employee at a residence, with a daily rainfall record of 3.14 inches measured at the Key West International Airport, just south of the area of significant street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low blow-out tides occurred during the early morning hours under strong offshore flow and along with low astronomical tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fernandina NOS tide gauge reported a blow-out astronomical low tide of -6.13 ft NAVD88 datum level.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blow-out astronomical tide was reported at the Mayport tide gauge of -4.92 ft datum level NAVD88.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rough surf and stronger than normal rip currents occurred during the first few days of January. A surfer drowned in the rough surf in Melbourne Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A stretch of above normal temperatures led to favorable conditions for bathers and surfers at local beaches. Several rip current rescues occurred during this holiday vacation period. On Sunday, January 4 at 1600 EST, a 64 year old famous surf photographer from Rhode Island was upended by rough waves while surfing and knocked unconscious at Paradise Beach in Melbourne Beach.  He never regained consciousness and died about a week later in the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High surf was produced by an Atlantic gale. A surfer was found unconscious in the surf in Cocoa Beach and brought to shore. He was taken to a local hospital and was later released.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front produced gale conditions in the Atlantic coastal waters on January 8 and caused seas to build to around 10 feet. Winds diminished the following day, but lingering swells continued to produce very rough surf conditions. A male surfer in his 60s was found unconscious and not breathing in the surf across from Patrick Air Force Base at 1045 EST. Bystanders returned him to shore, where a Patrick Air Force Base medical crew resuscitated him. He was transported to a local hospital in stable condition where he later recovered and was released.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered summertime convection developed during the afternoon hours with a few storms becoming severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A green cherry tree fell on power lines in southern Madison county causing a power outage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active west coast sea breeze merged with the east coast sea breeze and produced numerous thunderstorms over Brevard County. A lightning strike from one of the storms sparked a fire that ultimately destroyed a home in Viera.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home in Viera, causing a fire to break out that eventually destroyed the residence. A resident who was home at the time reported hearing a large crack of thunder and eventually smelled smoke coming from the attic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved to near Lake Okeechobee and then stalled. This caused numerous afternoon to early evening thunderstorms across the east coast region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report was received through social media of quarter sized hail in Pembroke Pines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the week of March 8th through the 14th, onshore flow along the Atlantic coast resulted in strong rip currents at the beaches. Beach attendance was heavy each day due to spring break with many rescues and a couple of drownings. The city of Hollywood reported 24 people rescued from Rip Currents and Pompano Beach reported 15 people were rescued ranging in ages from 6 to 69.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At around 345 PM EDT, a 21-year old male British national went missing in the surf at MacArthur Beach State Park in North Palm Beach. A teenage girl who was swimming with him and was also caught in a rip current but she was rescued by two college students who swim for Florida International University. She was taken to the hospital for treatment and was reported to be breathing normally. The body of the male was discovered on Sunday at around 12:30 PM EDT by the Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission at the same beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough meandered over southeast Georgia with a prevailing WSW flow in place. An unstable environment combined with forcing from the sea breezes and the trough produced scattered severe storms in the afternoon with wet downbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a semi tractor which caused a brief fire along Interstate 75 near mile marker 364. The time and location are approximate. The cost of damage was unknown but estimated for the event to be included in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon showers and thunderstorms developed along the I-75 corridor before gradually pushing towards the Gulf Coast. Thunderstorms produced gusty winds and heavy rain across Pasco County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio operator reported a large tree down near Main St. and Grand Blvd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms quickly developed across southwest Florida during the afternoon hours. Numerous lightning strikes were observed with these storms, one of which produced a lightning strike which killed one roof worker while injuring another.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two men working on a roof were struck by lightning. They stopped working when it began to rain but returned shortly after the rain had stopped. Neighbors said that the lightning strike hit a roofer directly in the head ��� knocking him unconscious. That bolt then transferred to another worker who was just steps away. They were transferred to a local hospital to be treated for their injuries. The man who was struck directly died from his injuries three days later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon showers and thunderstorms developed along the I-75 corridor before gradually pushing towards the Gulf Coast. Thunderstorms produced gusty winds and heavy rain across Pasco County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported power lines and a tree down across the intersection of Washington Rd. and Virginia Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north of the Altamaha River Basin in advance of an approaching cold front and parent upper level trough pushing downstream of the MS river valley. Modest instability and shear was in place with supported scattered severe storms. A waterspout was observed on Sante Fe Lake in Alachua County, but dissipated before making landfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power line was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon over Pinellas County as the sea breeze circulation setup. A lightning strike from one of these storms caused minor damage to a residence.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pinellas County Fire and Rescue reported that lightning caused minor damge to a residence in St. Petersburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure was resulting in onshore flow across the southeast coast and causing strong rip currents at the beaches throughout the Memorial Day weekend. Numerous rescues were performed by Ocean Rescue lifeguards along many southeast Florida beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency response crews at Lake Worth Beach searched and rescued a 16-year-old boy who went missing in the ocean. He was taken to a local hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure was resulting in onshore flow across the southeast coast and causing strong rip currents at the beaches throughout the Memorial Day weekend. Numerous rescues were performed by Ocean Rescue lifeguards along many southeast Florida beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Delray Beach lifeguards responded to four or five calls throughout the day, with three people requiring medical treatment. Two of the near-drownings were near Atlantic Avenue and South Ocean Boulevard and the third was across from Anchor Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure to the north was resulting in scattered showers over the Atlantic waters and moving onshore of the southeast coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fire Rescue and video report confirmed a waterspout came onshore Fort Lauderdale Beach around 1200 PM EDT. Inflatable bounce-house was lifted into the air. Four people were injured, including one adult and three children. One of the injuries was serious. The rating was EF0 with a maximum distance estimated to be 100 yards and maximum width estimated to be 10 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north of the Altamaha River Basin in advance of an approaching cold front and parent upper level trough pushing downstream of the MS river valley. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure was resulting in onshore flow across the southeast coast and causing strong rip currents at the beaches throughout the Memorial Day weekend. Numerous rescues were performed by Ocean Rescue lifeguards along many southeast Florida beaches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line ahead of advancing strong cold front moved across South Florida during the late afternoon and early evening. In addition to causing minor structural damage, approximately 5,000 customers lost power in Broward County and 3,000 in Miami-Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A building maintenance worker was killed after he was struck by a glass tabletop that flew off a 9th floor balcony of Flamingo Apartments in Miami Beach. The tabletop flew off during high winds from thunderstorms which affected the area. Nearest measured wind gust was 46 mph at the City of Miami Beach Police Headquarters at 656 PM EDT. It is likely winds were higher at the 9th floor of the building. Report was supplemented with information from City of Miami Beach Department of Emergency Management.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line ahead of advancing strong cold front moved across South Florida during the late afternoon and early evening. In addition to causing minor structural damage, approximately 5,000 customers lost power in Broward County and 3,000 in Miami-Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds measured by hand-held instrument as high as 71 mph near the Mall at Wellington Green.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line ahead of advancing strong cold front moved across South Florida during the late afternoon and early evening. In addition to causing minor structural damage, approximately 5,000 customers lost power in Broward County and 3,000 in Miami-Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 40' by 80' vendor tent at the Margate City Fair collapsed as strong winds from a thunderstorm outflow moved through the area. One person suffered minor injuries from a cut to his right foot. Measured winds in the general area at the time were 52 mph at Liberty Elementary School slightly over 1 mile from the fair location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually strong upper level winds for summer combined with high instability and steep mid level lapse rates to produce numerous reports of severe weather across the tri-state area.  Multiple rounds of severe weather occurred on June 30th, especially across the Florida Panhandle.  The city of Tallahassee reported that 160 tons of yard waste was generated from the severe weather, requiring extra work time to remove storm debris.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were blown down around Blountstown with widespread power outages in the city.  Only one restaurant and one gas station kept power.  Damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level area of low pressure along with a surface low off the Carolinas coast caused numerous strong thunderstorms over the southeast metropolitan area of South Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail was reported by an off duty NWS employee in The Crossings area of West Kendall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over the Atlantic with a trailing cold front across North Florida caused scattered afternoon thunderstorms along the southeast coast of the peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported at Silver Trail Middle School in Pembroke Pines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A collision of the west and east coast sea breezes generated a severe thunderstorm over Lake County that produced quarter-sized hail in Clermont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio operator reported quarter-sized hail just east of US-27 in Clermont about 3 miles south of State Road 50.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure was resulting in onshore flow across the southeast coast and causing strong rip currents at the beaches throughout the Memorial Day weekend. Numerous rescues were performed by Ocean Rescue lifeguards along many southeast Florida beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lifeguards and paramedics rescued a 24-year-old man at Ocean Reef Park in Singer Island. The man was unconscious but revived at the beach and taken to the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front over central Florida caused scattered afternoon thunderstorms across south Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of a waterspout coming onshore northwest of Belle Glade. Estimated strength was EF-0 with no damage reported. The duration was approximately 3 minutes. Times were estimated via radar. The tornado moved as far inland as the Hoover Dike but remained mostly inside the dike with no impact on populated areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A collision of the east and west coast sea breezes along with the Lake Apopka breeze lead to the development of severe storms across southern Lake County. The damaging winds knocked over several trees in the Ferndale-Clermont area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lake County Sherriff's Office reported several trees down along Sugarloaf Mountain Road in Clermont. Additional reports of downed trees in yards were relayed by the public to local media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable airmass caused scattered afternoon showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video obtained from West Palm Beach broadcast media showed a likely tornadic circulation at the County Line Plaza in Tequesta. Damage appeared to be limited to uprooted trees in the parking lot and roofing material. The roof at the shopping center was undergoing repair at the time of the tornado, therefore much of the roofing material was already detached from the structure. Water entered a business in the plaza from one of the sections where the roof was being repaired. This tornado eventually moved into the Intracoastal waterway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable airmass caused scattered afternoon showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report relayed via Broadcast Media of trees uprooted along El Portal Drive and Golfview Drive in the Tequesta Country Club. This was from the same storm that eventually produced the tornado/waterspout along US 1 and the Indian River along the county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture and weak southwest flow led to morning showers and storms over the coast that moved in towards the coast. A lightning strike from one of these storms sparked a house fire in Lakewood Ranch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported that a fire at a house in Lakewood Ranch was apparently sparked by a lightning strike. Damages were expected to exceed $100,000. Time was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved through the Florida peninsula during the afternoon and early evening hours. A lightning strike from one of these storms damaged a drinking water facility in Inverness.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Florida Division of Emergency Management relayed a report of a water treatment facility in Inverness that was damaged by a lightning strike, disrupting the drinking water supply to the town. As a result, a boil water notice went into effect and was not lifted until July 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable airmass resulted in scattered afternoon showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Collier County EM relayed report of sabal palm tree down on house in the 2700 block of 8th Street NW near Orange Tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable environment caused scattered afternoon showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house in the Aqualane Shores Neighborhood in Naples around 6 PM EDT. Fire-Rescue crews reported heavy smoke and fire coming out of the attic of the two-story house. It took crews close to three hours to extinguish the fire. The house sustained very heavy fire, smoke, heat and water damages with the losses estimated at close to $2 million dollars when factoring both structural and content losses. A firefighter was injured when a section of ceiling collapsed on the second floor, knocking him to the ground and causing shoulder and back injuries. He was transported to a local hospital.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down in Tequesta.

A total of 12,940 customers reported power outages in Miami-Dade County, with an additional 3,520 in Broward County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak area of low pressure developed along a stationary frontal boundary across north Florida. This allowed for waves of showers and thunderstorms to move across the area for a few days causing flooding throughout much of the Tampa Bay area. The heaviest rain fall on the morning of the 3rd with some portions of Hillsborough, Pinellas and Pasco Counties receiving 6 to 8 of rain. This event was exacerbated from the flooding and saturated soils from multiple heavy rain events that occurred on August 1 and again during the last week of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple days of heavy rain caused widespread road closures across the Tampa area. Dozens of roads were flooded including the major thoroughfares of Armenia Avenue, Dale Mabry Highway, Gandy Boulevard, Westshore Boulevard, Veteran's Expressway, US 41, and State Road 60. The flooding resulted in 117 road cave-ins across Tampa, 99 homes and 66 business were impacted by the flooding in the City. 9 City of Tampa vehicles were damaged in the flooding. Additionally, a large tree was uprooted due to the saturated ground and fell on a car just west of Bayshore Boulevard on South Gandy Boulevard.||Further north across parts of Temple Terrace, including the University of South Florida, there was additional damage from flooding. The university sustained $137K in damages while 101 homes in the Temple Terrace were impacted. 88 homes sustained major damage as did 7 mobile homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved through the Nature Coast during through the mid to late afternoon, producing damaging wind gusts in eastern Levy County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Williston Fire Rescue reported on a social media page that numerous trees and power lines were down across the area, with some of the trees down across roads and on houses. Pictures accompanying the post showed several large oak trees down. Time was estimated from radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Florida Emergency Management and local broadcast media relayed pictures of a funnel near Charlotte County Airport, with some pictures showing what appears to be the funnel touching the ground. No damage was reported. Location estimated from radar.

Additional minor debris littered the roadways across Hartwood Marsh Road.  The heaviest damage was reported to be off Lake Boulevard and Highway 50 near the Lake and Orange County line. Winds were estimated near 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable airmass resulted in scattered afternoon showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down and a flag pole bent in half at a summer camp. Two large tents were destroyed. Time was estimated by radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down at the intersection of Pine Ridge and Livingston Roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning associated with thunderstorms developing along the east coast sea breeze injured a boy fishing in the water at Daytona Beach Shores.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An eleven year old boy from Georgia was struck by lightning while fishing in the ocean in waist deep water. The boy was transported to a nearby hospital and admitted in serious condition. After several days in the hospital, he was released to a rehabilitation center and was recovering.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The late afternoon collision of the east and west coast sea breezes as well as other local lake and outflow boundaries led to the development of several severe storms across Metro Orlando. These storms downed trees and produced wind damage to several homes in Casselberry. Additional minor tree damage was reported across Orange County along with the partial roof collapse of a home in Orlando. These storms produced a strong outflow boundary that reached the Brevard County coast, where several funnel cloud reports were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported several brief funnel clouds along a strong outflow boundary as it moved offshore Cocoa Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon sea breeze storms and cool temperatures aloft once again set the stage for strong to severe storms across the Florida Peninsula. One storm produced a lightning strike which caused minor damage to a house.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tampa Fire Rescue reported that a roof was blown off a building at the intersection of 56th Street and Hanna Avenue in East Tampa. There were also several power lines down in the same area. Pea sized hail was also reported at the location.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike hit the chimney of a home located on Farrier Trail in Clearwater. Upon arrival, firefighters had to make some small holes in the ceiling of the home to investigate if anything was on fire or not. No fire resulted from the strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable airmass resulted in scattered afternoon showers and thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down in the Vineyards at Vanderbilt Beach Rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seminole County Emergency Management reported 23 mobile homes with roof, awning, and screen damage across Lake Kathryn Estates in Casselberry. Four of the homes suffered major roof damage, with three sustaining nearly complete roof damage. Numerous downed power lines, tree limbs, and fences were also reported within the community. A large tree fell on a nearby house just outside the subdivision. Also nearby, a trained spotter reported several downed trees blocking roadway near Secret Lake Park in Casselberry. Photos were provided by local media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The collision of the east and west coast sea breezes across the interior of east-central Florida led to the development of severe storms. Quarter sized hail was reported with storms in Eustis, and near Harmony  in Osceola County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gas station attendant in Harmony reported that a customer observed quarter sized hail while driving along U.S. Highway 192 near the station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually strong upper level winds for summer combined with high instability and steep mid level lapse rates to produce numerous reports of severe weather across the tri-state area.  Multiple rounds of severe weather occurred on June 30th, especially across the Florida Panhandle.  The city of Tallahassee reported that 160 tons of yard waste was generated from the severe weather, requiring extra work time to remove storm debris.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down down onto Jason Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms combined with cool temperatures aloft led to an ideal setup for strong thunderstorms capable of producing hail and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The large business sign at the Market 27 store in Avon Park was destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in southwest Florida during the late afternoon hours as they pushed towards the Gulf Coast. One thunderstorm produced gusty winds along Fort Myers Beach.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter near Fort Myers Beach reported estimated wind gusts near 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak area of low pressure developed along a stationary frontal boundary across north Florida. This allowed for waves of showers and thunderstorms to move across the area for a few days causing flooding throughout much of the Tampa Bay area. The heaviest rain fall on the morning of the 3rd with some portions of Hillsborough, Pinellas and Pasco Counties receiving 6 to 8 of rain. This event was exacerbated from the flooding and saturated soils from multiple heavy rain events that occurred on August 1 and again during the last week of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter near Hudson reported receiving 7.5 inches of rain since midnight. The rain soaked ground caused trees and power lines to fall in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms produced hundreds of lightning strikes across the Tampa Bay Area during the late evening on Sunday. One lightning strike hit a house in Land O' Lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike hit a house in Land O' Lakes and the subsequent fire caused the house to be a total loss. The fire caused the second story to collapse onto the first floor. The home was a vacation property and was vacant at the time so no injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous eastward moving thunderstorm crossed central Florida during the afternoon. One storm became strong and produced some very localized wind damage in Tavares. Another storm produced lightning which struck and injured two women in Orlando.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two women were struck by lightning in advance of an approaching storm, as they were standing outdoors in the driveway of a home in Orlando. Doctors believe one woman sustained a direct strike to her head. She was transported to a hospital in serious condition, but was able to be released the following day. The second woman was struck indirectly and refused medical treatment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous eastward moving thunderstorm crossed central Florida during the afternoon. One storm became strong and produced some very localized wind damage in Tavares. Another storm produced lightning which struck and injured two women in Orlando.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous branches were downed in Leesburg from a thunderstorm producing winds estimated near 50 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous eastward moving thunderstorm crossed central Florida during the afternoon. One storm became strong and produced some very localized wind damage in Tavares. Another storm produced lightning which struck and injured two women in Orlando.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a locally damaging microburst which downed one large and partially rotten oak tree in Tavares. The tree toppled onto a playground and damaged a fence and shed. Other small branches and twigs were downed in the immediate vicinity. Winds were estimated at 50 to 55 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed along a weak frontal boundary over the eastern Gulf of Mexico late in the week and stretched across the northern Florida Peninsula during the weekend. In combination with anomalously high atmospheric moisture and onshore flow, this low helped produce numerous showers and thunderstorms that caused very high rainfall rates and slow moving storms. In some cases, areas received several rounds of heavy rainfall over the course of a few days. This resulted in some areas receiving as over 11 inches of rain between the 24th and 27th, with both areal flooding and river flooding reported. One fatality occurred with the residual flooding in Pinellas County on the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pasco County Emergency Management and numerous broadcast media outlets reported widespread flooding across western Pasco County due to a couple of days of very heavy rainfall, with at least one site reporting over 11 inches of rain. ||In Hudson on the 24th, street flooding was reported to have caused several stranded cars, with water almost up to the hood of one car.||In New Port Richey, Jarvis Street had to be closed on the 24th due to the heavy rain, and water was over the height of car tires near the intersection of US 19 and State Road 54.||More rain fell on the already saturated ground on the 25th, making conditions worse. More street flooding, closed roads, and stranded cars were reported in New Port Richey, Port Richey, Trinity, and Hudson.

On the 26th, the San Carlos Park area near Estero had reports of neighborhood flooding, and water was reported above the seawalls along Hancock Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed along a weak frontal boundary over the eastern Gulf of Mexico late in the week and stretched across the northern Florida Peninsula during the weekend. In combination with anomalously high atmospheric moisture and onshore flow, this low helped produce numerous showers and thunderstorms that caused very high rainfall rates and slow moving storms. In some cases, areas received several rounds of heavy rainfall over the course of a few days. This resulted in some areas receiving as over 11 inches of rain between the 24th and 27th, with both areal flooding and river flooding reported. One fatality occurred with the residual flooding in Pinellas County on the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell over the Anclote River basin on the 25th and 26th, causing the river to rise into moderate flood stage (22 feet) during the evening of the 25th, cresting at a level of 23.62 feet early on the morning of the 27th, then finally falling back into minor flood stage on the 28th. The Pasco County Office of Emergency Management reported that 321 homes were evacuated near Elfers along the Anclote River, and multiple swift water rescues were conducted by Pasco County Fire Rescue.

One fatality occurred with the residual flooding in Pinellas County on the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pinellas County Emergency Management relayed a report from the Medical Examiner of a 58 year old homeless man who was found face down submerged in a drainage area that was filled with storm water on July 29th. The man was resuscitated and taken to a hospital where he eventually expired on August 4th. The Medical Examiner ruled that the cause of death was complications of drowning, with contributing conditions of arteriosclerotic cardiovascular disease and chronic ethanolism.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Anclote River near Elfers, was just below flood stage at the onset of the heavy rain courtesy of 6-10 of rain across the basin at the end of July. The heavy rain and subsequent river flooding at the beginning of August produced millions in dollars of damage across Pasco County. The river crest at 25.4 feet which was well above major flood stage of 24.0 feet. ||In total, 46 single family dwellings were destroyed, 32 received major damage and 258 homes sustained minor damage from the flooding. 36 mobile homes were destroyed while 6 others sustained damage from flooding.

He was treated at the scene by county fire rescue, then transported to a Jacksonville hospital where he was in serious condition. The extent of injuries were unknown.

A packing shed was destroyed, a potato field was damaged, and the tornado also destroyed a shed and damaged a metal constructed structure near 6106 County Road 305. Estimated winds were near 80 mph. The cost of damage was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface ridge axis was south of the region with a dominate west coast sea breeze regime and deep moisture in place from the Gulf Coast region. Storm coverage focused toward the I-95 corridor in the late afternoon and evening where damaging downburst winds occurred.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning hit the side of a house on Waxmyrtle Way in Naples. The resulting electrical current traveled through conduits to the attic, where extensive damage was reported. Two adults and two children were in the home at the time and were able to get out safely. The home was deemed unlivable at the time of the incident.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down at Boynton Beach Mall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mean layer trough axis from the surface to 500 mb carved out and persisted across the local area for a few days. A very moist airmass with precipitable water values at or above 2 inches under prevailing SW flow out of the Gulf of Mexico fueled scattered afternoon and early evening thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto a power line in High Springs. The cost of damage was unknown but estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the area with an unusually strong low-level jet.  In fact, this was a record breaking event in terms of upper air. The 62 knot wind measured by the TAE balloon at 850 mb ties the record for the strongest 850 mb wind measured by a Tallahassee balloon for either a 00z or 12z launch. Although an inversion was present, the airmass modified quickly, and the southeast big bend (usually a severe weather report minimum in the area) had a lot of trees and power lines down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto power lines near the intersection of Old St. Augustine Road and Southwood Plantation Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the area with an unusually strong low-level jet.  In fact, this was a record breaking event in terms of upper air. The 62 knot wind measured by the TAE balloon at 850 mb ties the record for the strongest 850 mb wind measured by a Tallahassee balloon for either a 00z or 12z launch. Although an inversion was present, the airmass modified quickly, and the southeast big bend (usually a severe weather report minimum in the area) had a lot of trees and power lines down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down across Lafayette county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary traveling ahead of a cold front produced a line of showers and thunderstorms on the evening of the 26th. A lightning strike from one of these storms struck a tree causing it to smoulder, which later spread to a nearby vacant house.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a tree late on the evening of the 26th, causing it to smoulder. When fire fighters arrived at the house at 4am the next morning, the house was fully engulfed in flames.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed early in the morning as a cold front and weak low pressure crossed the peninsula. A strong thunderstorm produced wind damage in St. Lucie County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds in association with a line of thunderstorms moved across St. Lucie County early Thursday morning. Straight line winds embedded in part of the line produced minor damage on Ulrich Road (between Oleander Avenue and US-1). Estimated winds of 50 to 60 mph produced damage to several large trees and one home sustained minor roof and soffit damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain associated with the intersection of a stalled frontal boundary and a coastal trough along the Treasure Coast trained into the coasts of St. Lucie and Martin Counties during the morning and early afternoon hours. Additional slow moving storms developed in the afternoon producing heavy rain and small hail in Central Okeechobee County. ||Twenty-four hour rainfall totals reached 8-11 inches in and around the Palm City and Stuart areas with 3-7 inch amounts around Port St. Lucie. Radar estimated rainfall totals were up to 7 inches across Central Okeechobee County. The majority of this rainfall occurred in a period of 6-hours or less. ||Flooding of many roadways occurred, stranding many vehicles. Water entered several homes in Palm City. Drainage canals overflowed and standing water reached a foot in many locations and was slow to recede. Standing water was reported on roadways in Central Okeechobee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow moving strong storm across Okeechobee County produced 5 to 7 inches of rain across the northwest and central portions of the county during the afternoon and evening, primarily over the Kissimmee Prairie Preserve. The COOP observer at the Kissimmee Prairie Preserve reported 5.69 inches of rain in 24 hours while another range gauge at the park reported 6.92 inches. Most of this rainfall fell in less than 6 hours.||This heavy rainfall led to minor flooding of roadways across Central Okeechobee County. The Kissimmee Prairie observer reported several inches of standing water on local roadways, which remained passable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain associated with the intersection of a stalled frontal boundary and a coastal trough along the Treasure Coast trained into the coasts of St. Lucie and Martin Counties during the morning and early afternoon hours. Additional slow moving storms developed in the afternoon producing heavy rain and small hail in Central Okeechobee County. ||Twenty-four hour rainfall totals reached 8-11 inches in and around the Palm City and Stuart areas with 3-7 inch amounts around Port St. Lucie. Radar estimated rainfall totals were up to 7 inches across Central Okeechobee County. The majority of this rainfall occurred in a period of 6-hours or less. ||Flooding of many roadways occurred, stranding many vehicles. Water entered several homes in Palm City. Drainage canals overflowed and standing water reached a foot in many locations and was slow to recede. Standing water was reported on roadways in Central Okeechobee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of CoCoRAaS, ham radio and public reports indicated 5 to 11 inches of rain fell across northeastern Martin County, with most of the rain falling in a 6-hour or less period. The highest totals occurred within the southwestern portion of Stuart and in Palm City. ||Flooding closed many roadways, stranding over 100 vehicles. Drainage canals and creeks overflowed. While high water surrounded many subdivisions, businesses and homes, water was reported to have entered 7 homes in Palm City. Damage was estimated around $105,000 dollars.

The majority of this rainfall occurred in a period of 6-hours or less. ||Flooding of many roadways occurred, stranding many vehicles. Water entered several homes in Palm City. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A combination of CoCoRaHS, ham radio, and public reports indicated 3 to 6 inches of rain fell across southeastern St. Lucie County, with most of the rain falling in a 6-hour or less period. ||Flooding closed many primary and secondary roadways, stranding and submerging many vehicles. Drainage canals and creeks overflowed. While high water surrounded many subdivisions, businesses and homes, no water was reported to have entered any homes or businesses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 17-year old male was swimming outside the guarded beach at Jaycee Beach Park in Vero Beach. He was caught within a rip current and drowned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 17-year old male from the local area was swimming outside the guarded beach at Jaycee Beach Park in Vero Beach. Rip currents, rough surf and a south-to-north longshore current were present throughout the day. The swimmer was likely caught within a rip current around 1600 EST and drowned. His body was recovered in the surf south of Jaycee Beach Park two days later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm intensified as it moved east over St. Lucie County and interacted with the east coast sea breeze. The thunderstorm produced strong winds that toppled a few trees onto homes as well as half-dollar sized hail in Fort Pierce.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: St. Lucie County Emergency Management officials reported half-dollar sized hail on U.S. Highway 1 between Weatherbee and Tumblin Kling Roads as a severe thunderstorm moved through Fort Pierce.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated, pulse severe thunderstorms developed across northwest Florida during the afternoon and early evening of June 23, 2015.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported just south of Crestview.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated, pulse severe thunderstorms developed across northwest Florida during the afternoon and early evening of June 23, 2015.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KEGI measured a 61 mph wind gust in association with a severe thunderstorm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house, resulting in roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm intensified as it moved east over St. Lucie County and interacted with the east coast sea breeze. The thunderstorm produced strong winds that toppled a few trees onto homes as well as half-dollar sized hail in Fort Pierce.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated wind gusts between 60 and 65 mph caused numerous trees and branches to break and fall in the 300 block of East Weatherbee Road and 300 block of Tumblin Kling Road, according to St. Lucie County Emergency Management. A tree fell on a carport and another fell on a home, briefly trapping an unknown number of people inside. At a nearby car dealership, several vehicles on display with their hoods raised sustained damage and several display windows were broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an upper level disturbance, weak cold front, and high levels of moisture and instability resulted in the development of a line of thunderstorms that moved northwest to southeast across the area. One of the storms became severe across Okaloosa County during the afternoon of June 27th, with lightning damage occurring in Santa Rosa County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a business off Homer Holland Rd. The business suffered smoke damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms moved through the Florida peninsula ahead of a cold front. A couple of these storms produced damaging downburst wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed pictures of trees down near Harns Marsh in Lehigh Acres. Most of the trees down were shallow rooted in a wet marsh area, and a nearby ASOS as Regional Southwest Airport recorded wind gusts of 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon seabreeze showers and thunderstorms developed from the Bay Area into southwest Florida. Although most stayed below severe levels, one was able to produce quarter size hail across Lee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service in Miami relayed a report from a Collier County Deputy located near Bonita Beach. Hail was falling at the intersections of Bonita Beach Blvd and Tamiami Trail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved through the Florida peninsula ahead of a cold front early on the morning of the 24th. One of these storms caused a short lived tornado in southern Sarasota County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Myakka State Forest Ranger reported tornado damage to a ranger station and a mobile home trailer at around 3:50 am.  A subsequent NWS storm survey found a 50 yard wide, one mile long intermittent path of damage that included snapped and uprooted trees and a mobile home trailer that had been picked up and tossed 30 to 40 yards. The damage was found to be consistent with an EF-0 tornado making multiple touchdowns with estimated peak winds of 85 mph. The survey found low end EF-0 damage to the east of the Myakka River that included minor roof damage, a bent stop sign pole, and numerous broken tree branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across South Florida with showers and severe thunderstorms ahead of the front. One of these severe thunderstorms spawned a tornado in Boca Raton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Broward County Sheriff's deputy who was directing traffic in Tamarac was injured after lightning struck a nearby utility box. The lightning struck an Florida Power & Light signal box at the corner of the intersection of University Drive and Southgate Boulevard and that transferred some residual electricity to the deputy.||The deputy felt a tingling and was transported to a nearby hospital for examination. Time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary front was located across South Florida with a surface trough along the southeast coast of the peninsula causing a strong thunderstorms to form. Persistent rains also led to flooding in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media reports estimated winds at the PGA National Golf Course reached 60 mph during the third round of the PGA Tour's Honda Classic. At least one large tree was uprooted on the course, with minor damage to tents and other outdoor items.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quasi-stationary front was located across South Florida with a surface trough along the southeast coast of the peninsula causing a strong thunderstorms to form. Persistent rains also led to flooding in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding occurred in the Omni, Edgewater and Midtown areas of the City of Miami, mainly along the Biscayne Boulevard and North Miami Avenue corridors. Multiple cars stalled and flowing water was up to a foot deep. Media reported minor inundation of businesses on the north edge of downtown. Multiple businesses in the Miami Midtown Shops closed as their ground floors were flooded.  Survey of debris lines indicated that most businesses along the Biscayne Boulevard corridor between 195 and 395 had water above the base of their front doors.||Damage total is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across South Florida with showers and severe thunderstorms ahead of the front. One of these severe thunderstorms spawned a tornado in Boca Raton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado which had initially touched down at the Woodfield Hunt Club community in West Boca touched down again in The Colonnade community, then continued on a ENE track across St. Andrews Blvd and affecting the Les Jardins community, then lifting at Patch Reef Park south of Yamato Road and west of Military Trail. ||Damage in this segment represented EF-0 magnitude and consisted mainly of roof damage, downed trees and branches and light poles (especially at Patch Reef Park). A light pole at the park fell on a city vehicle. Total damage to park was around $8,000 and included the light pole and the windshield of the city vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across South Florida with showers and severe thunderstorms ahead of the front. One of these severe thunderstorms spawned a tornado in Boca Raton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down in the Woodfield Hunt Club community of Boca Raton at 736 AM EST. Damage consisted of several uprooted trees, several large trees snapped halfway up and a tree felled into a house. Roofing was damaged from downed trees and limbs and strong winds also removed numerous Spanish tiles from many homes. Of note was a projectile in the form of a 6-12 inch diameter tree limb which punctured a roof. The tree limb was likely carried by the tornado from an adjacent neighborhood. ||The tornado lifted just west of Jog Road and touched down again on the east side of Jog Road. This segment of the tornado was rated an EF-1. Damage total is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating combined with a potent upper level system allowed for strong to severe storms to develop throughout the area. Many were sub-severe however a few reached severe limits causing damage to homes, trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local broadcast media reported several downed trees around Starkey Rd. in Seminole.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms moved through the Florida peninsula ahead of a cold front. A couple of these storms produced damaging downburst wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A broadcast media relayed pictures of wind damage to a building at a fruit farm in Bradenton. Damage included a section of the roof peeled off the building allowing rain to get inside, a metal awning knocked down, and a metal gate damaged was bent. Time was estimated based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across the region on April 19th, bringing with it tornadoes and straight line wind damage.  Then on April 20th, another line of severe storms moved from Gulf county eastward through Tallahassee, bringing down numerous trees and power lines along its path, especially in Tallahassee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sporadic tree damage was found along a well defined path.  Maximum winds were estimated to be around 95 mph with this EF1 tornado.  The most focused area of damage was around Meridian Road and Maclay Road.  No significant structural damage was noted, although some fence damage occurred on private property and one vehicle was crushed by falling trees.  A debris signature was noted on the KTLH dual-pol radar at the time and location of the damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the week of March 8th through the 14th, onshore flow along the Atlantic coast resulted in strong rip currents at the beaches. Beach attendance was heavy each day due to spring break with many rescues and a couple of drownings. The city of Hollywood reported 24 people rescued from Rip Currents and Pompano Beach reported 15 people were rescued ranging in ages from 6 to 69.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A middle aged man was pulled from the surf at about 3 PM EDT by lifeguards between 62nd and 63rd Streets on Miami Beach. He was taken to Mt. Sinai Hospital and died later in the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the week of March 8th through the 14th, onshore flow along the Atlantic coast resulted in strong rip currents at the beaches. Beach attendance was heavy each day due to spring break with many rescues and a couple of drownings. The city of Hollywood reported 24 people rescued from Rip Currents and Pompano Beach reported 15 people were rescued ranging in ages from 6 to 69.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Delray Beach, five distressed swimmers were pulled from the water and taken to the hospital. One male, about 55-years old, was in critical condition while the other four were listed in stable condition.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well defined bow echo moved east out of coastal Alabama into the western Florida Panhandle during the late afternoon hours of April 25. The produced extensive high winds and areas of damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated at 70 mph uprooted a large diameter tree and caused minor damage to a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across the region on April 19th, bringing with it tornadoes and straight line wind damage.  Then on April 20th, another line of severe storms moved from Gulf county eastward through Tallahassee, bringing down numerous trees and power lines along its path, especially in Tallahassee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado was confirmed by a KEVX dual-pol radar debris signature in a rural area west of Bruce.  Damage was likely confined to trees with no structural damage reported.  This tornado was rated EF0.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across the region on April 19th, bringing with it tornadoes and straight line wind damage.  Then on April 20th, another line of severe storms moved from Gulf county eastward through Tallahassee, bringing down numerous trees and power lines along its path, especially in Tallahassee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado traveled across three states from Jackson county, FL, through Houston county, AL, and ended in Early county, GA.  From the initial touchdown in Florida through the first few miles of the track into Houston county, AL, the path width varied from 100-200 yards wide.  Damage in the Florida part of the track was primarily limited to trees and power lines with a rating of EF1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Enhanced onshore flow created rough surf and numerous strong rip currents that kept local beach patrols extremely busy during the Memorial Day holiday weekend. Beach safety and local news media indicated four rip current related drownings and over 500 ocean rescues, primarily in Volusia and Brevard Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 43-year-old male tourist from Tampa died after being pulled unconscious from rough surf in Daytona Beach Shores around 1100 EST on May 24. One would be rescuer was taken to the hospital and listed in stable condition. Beach patrol indicated there were strong rip currents at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture and southwest flow produced heavy rain across the area. One cluster of thunderstorms trained over the Tampa Bay area during the early afternoon, dropping several inches of water and causing minor flooding. Multiple sites reported over 6 inches of rain from the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms caused heavy rainfall over Tampa, with several sites reporting over 6 inches.||Hillsborough County Sheriff's Office reported flooding on Hanna Avenue at 56th Street, with several stalled vehicles on the roadway.||Multiple street closures due to flooding were reported on broadcast media social media sites. Trained spotters also reported cars floating down the road near the intersection of Ferdinand Avenue and Tacon Street after trying to cross flooded roadways.||A trained spotter on MacDill Air Force Base estimated that approximately 90 percent of the roads on base were flooded.||Florida Department of Emergency Management reported that heavy rain and flooding closed the bridge to Davis Island. As a result, Tampa General Hospital was on bypass for over an hour due to the fact that emergency vehicles could not get to or from the hospital.||Damage was roughly estimated to be $200,000, mostly from flooded cars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak easterly flow led to a sea breeze collision over the western half of the Florida peninsula, generating scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening hours. A few of these storms produced hail up to 1 inch in diameter and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Levy County Department of Public Safety sent pictures of wind damage to a metal porch roof and damage to a barn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Enhanced onshore flow created rough surf and numerous strong rip currents that kept local beach patrols extremely busy during the Memorial Day holiday weekend. Beach safety and local news media indicated four rip current related drownings and over 500 ocean rescues, primarily in Volusia and Brevard Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 32-year-old male from Fellsmere drowned while rescuing his younger cousin from a rip current north of the Sebastian Inlet Jetty. He apparently went under after successfully pushing the younger male, who was uninjured, out of the rip current. The older male was pulled from the water by two surfers around 1750 EST on May 25 and taken to a trauma center, where he was later pronounced dead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Enhanced onshore flow created rough surf and numerous strong rip currents that kept local beach patrols extremely busy during the Memorial Day holiday weekend. Beach safety and local news media indicated four rip current related drownings and over 500 ocean rescues, primarily in Volusia and Brevard Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Volusia County Beach Patrol confirmed the death of a 62-year-old male from Lebanon who died after being rescued from a rip current near the 1700 block of Atlantic Avenue in Ormond Beach around 0955 EST on May 25. The man was unconscious after being pulled from the surf and later died at the hospital. Nearby at Daytona Beach, three people were rescued from rough surf and rip currents and transported to a local hospital.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on a house on Castleberry Drive.  Damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow and deep moisture brought a line of thunderstorms crossing the Florida peninsula during the afternoon hours. A couple of these storms produced damaging wind gusts across central Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Polk County Department of Emergency Management reported that a roof was completely blown off a mobile home on Jana Circle, and then was blown down the street, hitting a fence and power pole. Additionally, a power pole was blown down onto a house on Pine Street just south of downtown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Enhanced onshore flow created rough surf and numerous strong rip currents that kept local beach patrols extremely busy during the Memorial Day holiday weekend. Beach safety and local news media indicated four rip current related drownings and over 500 ocean rescues, primarily in Volusia and Brevard Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The body of a 50-year-old male from Kissimmee was pulled from the surf near the 700 block of Atlantic Street in Melbourne Beach around 1130 EST on May 25. Brevard County Fire Rescue and Beach Patrol noted there were strong rip currents in the area at the time. Two others were rescued from rough surf and rip currents to the north at Playalinda Beach and one person was rescued to the south at Hightower Beach in Satellite Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong easterly flow caused rough surf and strong rip currents late in the month. Several dozen rip current related rescues and two drownings were reported along the Volusia County coastline.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 53-year old male from Kissimmee was pulled from the surf at Daytona Beach around 1430 EST on May 31 and died an hour later in the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong easterly flow caused rough surf and strong rip currents late in the month. Several dozen rip current related rescues and two drownings were reported along the Volusia County coastline.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 17-year-old male from Georgia disappeared in the surf at New Smyrna Beach around 1600 EST on May 31. His body washed ashore the following day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along the west coast sea breeze and one became severe as it moved over Mount Dora in Lake County, producing damage to trees, signs, and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated wind gusts around 60 mph caused about a half-dozen trees as well as several signs and power lines to topple in the Mount Dora area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Easterly flow continued to provide late afternoon and early evening thunderstorms across the Florida west coast. One storm produced hail and gusty winds in St. Petersburg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained Skywarn spotter reported nickel size hail falling near the intersection of 30th Ave. and 66th Street near Pinellas Park. His spotter ID was PIN-631A.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture and weak easterly flow caused numerous afternoon thunderstorms over the Florida peninsula. A couple of these storms produced damaging wind gusts and small hail in Polk County, as well as a lightning strike that damaged a moving car.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A broadcast media outlet relayed reports and pictures of several large trees uprooted by thunderstorm wind gusts in Lakeland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture and weak easterly flow caused numerous afternoon thunderstorms over the Florida peninsula. A couple of these storms produced damaging wind gusts and small hail in Polk County, as well as a lightning strike that damaged a moving car.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A broadcast media outlet reported that a car in one of the east bound lanes of Interstate 4 was apparently struck by lightning near Polk City. The driver reported two of the air bags were deployed, the glove box popped open, and power shut down to the car at the time of a bright flash during a thunderstorm. Several burn marks were later found on the car. The driver suffered bruises to her ribcage from the airbags.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow and deep moisture brought a line of thunderstorms crossing the Florida peninsula during the afternoon hours. A couple of these storms produced damaging wind gusts across central Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a large oak tree knocked down by thunderstorm wind gusts in Largo Central Park. Several reporting sites in the area recorded wind gusts of up to 45 knots. Time was estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep layer ridging was building westward from the Bahamas, which brought more mid level dry air over the local area. Dry air entrainment into scattered sea breeze and outflow driven thunderstorms increased downdraft magnitudes and caused widespread wind damage in severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down. The cost of damage was unknown, but estimated for the event to be included in StormData (likely an underestimate).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over the mainland and became severe as they interacted with the east coast sea breeze over central Volusia and eastern Seminole County. Large hail, up to quarter-sized, was reported across sections of Deltona and Oviedo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports were received from the public via social media of quarter-sized hail in Oviedo, and nearby, nickel-sized hail was reported at the Twin River Golf Club.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered convection developed across the tri-state area with isolated strong to severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two tree and a power line were blown down about a quarter mile north of Bonifay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered convection developed across the tri-state area with isolated strong to severe weather.
A well defined bow echo moved east out of coastal Alabama into the western Florida Panhandle during the late afternoon hours of April 25. The produced extensive high winds and areas of damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pensacola Regional Airport recorded a 61 mph wind gust at 405 pm.||The severe thunderstorm winds resulted in numerous trees being downed through the city of Pensacola. The downed trees also resulted in damage to some residential fences and a few homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well defined bow echo moved east out of coastal Alabama into the western Florida Panhandle during the late afternoon hours of April 25. The produced extensive high winds and areas of damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorm wind gusts downed several trees in the city of Pace.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well defined bow echo moved east out of coastal Alabama into the western Florida Panhandle during the late afternoon hours of April 25. The produced extensive high winds and areas of damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observation site at Whiting Field Naval Air Station recorded a wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical airmass across south Florida resulted in scattered afternoon thunderstorms with an isolated severe thunderstorm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported quarter sized hail in Miami Springs. Time was estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough over the central United States and an approaching cold front resulted in numerous showers and thunderstorms across the north central Gulf Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a house in the Florida panhandle causing damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance that tracked from the Lower Mississippi Valley into the Tennessee Valley resulted in scattered thunderstorms across the Deep South and an isolated instance of severe weather in the western Florida Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated at 60 mph blew trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A continued weak easterly flow led to a sea breeze collision over the western half of the Florida peninsula, generating scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening hours that moved west towards the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cape Coral Police Department reported several downed trees and power lines in Cape Coral and just south of the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A continued weak easterly flow led to a sea breeze collision over the western half of the Florida peninsula, generating scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening hours that moved west towards the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sumter County Sheriffs office reported two downed trees along County Road 647 in rural areas just east of Nobleton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A continued weak easterly flow led to a sea breeze collision over the western half of the Florida peninsula, generating scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening hours that moved west towards the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A caller reported quarter size hail falling at the intersection of Gunn Hwy and Anderson Road near Citrus Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A continued weak easterly flow led to a sea breeze collision over the western half of the Florida peninsula, generating scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening hours that moved west towards the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at Peter O. Knight Airport recorded a 52 knot thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line ahead of advancing strong cold front moved across South Florida during the late afternoon and early evening. In addition to causing minor structural damage, approximately 5,000 customers lost power in Broward County and 3,000 in Miami-Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph was measured by a Weatherbug station at Sawgrass Springs Middle School in Coral Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed throughout central Florida in a summer-like weather pattern. One of the storms became severe as it moved off Apopka, downing several large trees and producing minor structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Apopka Police, Orange County Fire Rescue, and numerous pictures provided via local television stations on social media indicated numerous fallen trees and minor structural damage in Apopka from estimated winds between 60 and 65 mph. On the 2300 Block of Highland Avenue, a large tree fell on a car traveling down the road; however, its occupants were uninjured. Several large trees were downed in and around the Errol Estates subdivision. A large roadside billboard was toppled and a church lost several large metal sheets from its roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fast moving thunderstorms developed in a warm and moist atmosphere ahead of a cold front over northern Florida. A few of the storms became severe and produced multiple rounds of large hail across Okeechobee and Martin Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports via broadcast media and social media of quarter-sized hail in the city of Okeechobee. According to reports, duration of the hail was approximately 10 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A continued weak easterly flow led to a sea breeze collision over the western half of the Florida peninsula, generating scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening hours that moved west towards the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pinellas County Emergency Management relayed a report of a roof to an apartment building that collapsed under the weight of heavy rain pooling on the roof. The roof was under construction at the time of the incident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line ahead of advancing strong cold front moved across South Florida during the late afternoon and early evening. In addition to causing minor structural damage, approximately 5,000 customers lost power in Broward County and 3,000 in Miami-Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree branch approximately 8 inches in diameter snapped off a tree. The report was received via CBS12 Twitter feed. Time is approximate and based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line ahead of advancing strong cold front moved across South Florida during the late afternoon and early evening. In addition to causing minor structural damage, approximately 5,000 customers lost power in Broward County and 3,000 in Miami-Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts as high as 65 mph were recorded by a hand-held instrument along US 27 near Okeelanta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fast moving thunderstorms developed in a warm and moist atmosphere ahead of a cold front over northern Florida. A few of the storms became severe and produced multiple rounds of large hail across Okeechobee and Martin Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a swath of large hail reports across parts of Martin County including Palm City, Port Salerno, and Hobe Sound. Numerous reports of quarter to golf ball sized hailstones were reported across the area. Several pictures provided via social media showed hail accumulating on surfaces in Palm City, with depths generally below one-inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fast moving thunderstorms developed in a warm and moist atmosphere ahead of a cold front over northern Florida. A few of the storms became severe and produced multiple rounds of large hail across Okeechobee and Martin Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed report from public of golf ball-sized hail that fell in the Ancient Oaks subdivision along US-441 southeast of Taylor Creek. The public estimated that the hail fell for approximately 8 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered convection developed across the tri-state area with isolated strong to severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public estimated severe winds a few miles east of the Tallahassee airport.  The Tallahassee airport measured a wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually strong upper level winds for summer combined with high instability and steep mid level lapse rates to produce numerous reports of severe weather across the tri-state area.  Multiple rounds of severe weather occurred on June 30th, especially across the Florida Panhandle.  The city of Tallahassee reported that 160 tons of yard waste was generated from the severe weather, requiring extra work time to remove storm debris.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on NE Mason Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report was received from the public of nickel to quarter sized hail and heavy rain near Temple Terrace.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak easterly flow led to a sea breeze collision over the western half of the Florida peninsula, generating scattered thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening hours. A few of these storms produced hail up to 1 inch in diameter and damaging wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong east coast sea breeze moved west across the peninsula and collided with an outflow boundary over Polk County, producing a strong thunderstorm that dropped quarter sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Polk County Emergency Management relayed a report of quarter sized hail near Crooked Lake Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Steep mid level lapse rates and moderate amounts of deep layer shear combined to produce isolated to scattered thunderstorms across the area with large hail reported in Altha.

Strong diurnal heating and ample moisture allowed for an isolated severe thunderstorm to develop along a lingering outflow boundary across Highlands County. The storm produced gusty winds and large hail across much of Highlands County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Highlands County Sheriffs office reported numerous downed power lines in Lake Placid.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pictures and video were received via social media of quarter to golf ball size hail falling in Lake Placid.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm formed ahead of an approaching boundary and produced a few minutes of sub-severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported penny to nickel size hail falling near Rotunda. The hail lasted for one minute in duration.

The fire burned through a ceiling at one point and triggered the sprinklers, extinguishing the fire. The time of the lightning strike was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms tracked across the region on April 25th. Numerous reports of downed trees and power lines were received. This damage was scattered across a wide area with many counties affected. It is likely a widespread area saw wind gusts in excess of 60 mph with localized areas seeing wind gusts in excess of hurricane force based on the reports received and radar wind velocity data.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down with traffic being re-routed.  This report was relayed by a WMBB-TV meteorologist.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms developed over the mainland and became severe as they interacted with the east coast sea breeze over central Volusia and eastern Seminole County. Large hail, up to quarter-sized, was reported across sections of Deltona and Oviedo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter and the public reported quarter-sized hail in Deltona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm formed along the sea breeze and a southward shifting frontal boundary across Flagler county. A passing upper level short wave trough combined with the surface convergence and diurnal instability over Flagler which resulted in some hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported near Interstate 95.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front combined with passing upper level short wave trough energy fueled scattered pre-frontal storms. Cool upper level temperatures combined with diurnal instability fueld a lone severe storm over Columbia county that produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An airport employee reported nickel size hail at the Lake City airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderate risk of rip currents was in effect with surf advertised at 2-3 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 32 year old male died in the surf.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed in an unusually warm and unstable airmass. Several clusters of strong thunderstorms were moving offshore Brevard County and one storm became severe as it pushed over Cape Canaveral.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: USAF tower 0421 recorded a 54 knot wind gust out of the southwest as a severe thunderstorm pushed through. Nearby USAF tower 0422 recorded a 50 knot wind gust out of the southwest at 1735 EST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A WeatherFlow station in the Banana River north of the State Road 520 Causeway recorded a 59 mph wind gust out of the west as a severe thunderstorm moved over Merritt Island.
