EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north of the Altamaha River basin placing the entire forecast area with the warm sector of an approaching mid level trough.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down across Horseshoe Circle. The county road department removed the debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow and deep moisture produced several thunderstorms moving up the west coast of the Florida peninsula during the afternoon hours. Wind gusts from these storms caused minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a picture from a viewer of a large tree branch that was knocked down by a lightning strike near the intersection of Highway 54 and Highway 19 just before 7 pm EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow and deep moisture produced several thunderstorms moving up the west coast of the Florida peninsula during the afternoon hours. Wind gusts from these storms caused minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Charlotte County Emergency Management forwarded pictures of a metal porch ripped off a home in Rotonda.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow and deep moisture produced several thunderstorms moving up the west coast of the Florida peninsula during the afternoon hours. Wind gusts from these storms caused minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported her in-ground aluminum flag pole snapped in half and a wind anemometer blew away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow and deep moisture produced several thunderstorms moving up the west coast of the Florida peninsula during the afternoon hours. Wind gusts from these storms caused minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a report and picture of a large sign for a car repair business damaged by wind gusts. Time was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms developed along the sea breeze collision over the Nature Coast. One of these storms in Citrus County produced a severe wind gust and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS spotter reported hail the size of quarters 4.7 miles east-southeast of Crystal River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms developed along the sea breeze collision over the Nature Coast. One of these storms in Citrus County produced a severe wind gust and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at Crystal River Airport (KCGC) recorded a 61 knot thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level trough of low pressure over the Florida peninsula caused scattered afternoon strong thunderstorms and an isolated severe storm across Miami-Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime sized hail was reported in Richmond Heights.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over the Atlantic with a trailing cold front across North Florida caused scattered afternoon thunderstorms along the southeast coast of the peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime sized hail was reported at the intersection of Interstate 75 and Griffin Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure over the Atlantic with a trailing cold front across North Florida caused scattered afternoon thunderstorms along the southeast coast of the peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS employee reported quarter sized hail at the intersection of 189th NW Avenue and Taft Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A collision of the west and east coast sea breezes over the mainland of Brevard County sparked numerous thunderstorms that produced locally damaging wind gusts as they pushed east to the coast at Cocoa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts associated with a line of strong to severe thunderstorms blew across a series of small ponds in Cocoa and caused minor damage to a few mobile homes. Aluminum siding was blown downstream and partially wrapped around a telephone pole. Winds were estimated near 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A meandering surface front over the area in concert with the sea breezes and an approaching mid level impulse spawned scattered afternoon thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike in Macclenny injured two. A structure fire was also caused. The cost of damage was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A meandering surface front over the area in concert with the sea breezes and an approaching mid level impulse spawned scattered afternoon thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on a home on Southwest Pinemount Road. The cost of damage was unknown, but estimated for the event to be included in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered afternoon sea breeze storms impacted the area. One storm caused a tree to be blown down in Clay county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down on a power line at the intersection of Moody Avenue and Suzanne Avenue. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A meandering surface front over the area in concert with the sea breezes and an approaching mid level impulse spawned scattered afternoon thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down at Royal Palms Parkway and Rymfire Drive. The cost of damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along sea breeze boundaries, with the strongest storms moving through the Tampa Bay region during the late afternoon. The strongest storm caused damaging wind gusts and nickel sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS employee reported 2 large trees down around Providence Road in Brandon. Time was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along sea breeze boundaries, with the strongest storms moving through the Tampa Bay region during the late afternoon. The strongest storm caused damaging wind gusts and nickel sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a report of nickel sized hail near the intersection of Providence Road and Bloomingdale Avenue in Brandon. Time was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak flow and deep moisture produced late morning thunderstorms over Pinellas county. A lightning bolt from one of these storms struck and killed a man in Largo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Florida Emergency Management reported that an 81 year old man was struck and killed while walking near his home in Largo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along sea breeze boundaries along the west coast of central Florida during the early afternoon. One of these storms caused downburst winds that uprooted or snapped several large trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a report and pictures of several large trees uprooted or snapped at the bases near Nokomis, FL. The trees were all in a confined area and appeared to be blown down in roughly the same direction (to the west), leading to the conclusion that the damage was caused by a small microburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture allowed for sea breeze thunderstorms to develop and move across the area during the afternoon and early evening hours. The strongest of these storms produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a picture from a viewer of a wood patio and screened enclosure that was ripped off the back of the house. A large part of the decking came to rest on the roof of the house, and part of the screen enclosure ended up in a tree. Time was estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Building high pressure over Florida led to moderate east winds and a high risk of rip currents at the Atlantic beaches of southeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two women in their 20s drowned as a result of rip currents at an unguarded beach near 54th Street on Miami Beach. One woman died shortly after arrival at the hospital and the second died at the hospital four days later. The women were visiting the area from New York.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent moderate to strong east wind continued to produce strong rip currents at the southeast Florida beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man in his late 40s or early 50s was rescued by lifeguards at the beach behind the Palm Beach Shores Resort and Vacation Villas on Singer Island. The man was taken to a local hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent moderate to strong east wind continued to produce strong rip currents at the southeast Florida beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 29-year-old man was rescued from rip currents at the municipal beach on Singer Island and was taken to the hospital with chest pains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of severe storms preceded a cold front as it crossed the local area. Hail and gusty winds were the main convective hazards, although a discrete tornado did impact the Palm Cay subdivision in southern Marion County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado touched down just east of State Road 200 at the Oak Run Country Club. Spotty EF0 damage was noted throughout the Oak Run neighborhood. The tornado continued on an east to east-northeast path into the Palm Cay subdivision, where it reached its maximum intensity of EF1. Spotter EF0 damage was noted just south of SW 103rd Street at the conclusion of the tornado path, which was about 2 miles in length. The duration of this tornado was likely less than 5 minutes. There was significant roof and tree damage within the small EF1 damage. The cost of damage was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered pre-frontal severe thunderstorms impacted NE Florida during the afternoon and evening hours. Hail, wind damage and a lone tornado in Duval county occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado moved ashore and pushed just south of Hendricks Elementary snapping trees and power lines. Several structures were impacted by falling trees and limbs. Initial damage was tightly clustered with a path of approximately 200 yards. As the tornado crossed Phillips Highway several businesses sustained roof damage. Trailers in the Pine Oaks mobile home park were damaged as well. The tornado began to track more southeast as it passed I-95 and widened to 350 yards as it neared Englewood High School. At about 5:58 pm, the tornado began to weaken and dissipate. A wide swath of straight line winds continued to cause damage farther to the south and east of the path until shortly after 6 pm. The path width varied from 200-350 yards along a 3.45 mile path. The tornado lasted 5-6 minutes with the maximum wind speeds reached 104 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent onshore flow combined with elevated Atlantic water levels due to much higher than normal astronomical tides with the approach and passage of the Super Harvest Blood Moon (occurred on Sept. 27) funneled a lot of water into the St. Johns River basin. Elevated water levels, some the highest experienced since the 2004 tropical storms (Jeanne and Frances) caused flooding of low lying areas and some property. One of the hardest hit areas were near Dunns Creek, Welaka and Satsuma in Putnam county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Coastal flooding impacted the backyard of a home on the St. Johns River near Colee Cove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A remnant frontal boundary lingering over South Florida led to scattered showers and thunderstorms during the day. The slow moving storms lingered across locations in Miami-Dade and Broward counties, producing locally heavy rainfall and roadway flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road flooding resulted in the closure of NE 48th Street between NE 3rd Avenue and North Dixie Highway in Pompano Beach. Nearby rain gauges measured between 3 and 4 inches of rain in a short time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher than normal tides coupled with the approach of the new moon brought minor coastal flooding to vulnerable portions of Miami Beach during high tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Miami Beach officials reported tidal flooding along Indian Creek Drive (A1A) at 29th street with the morning high tide. Additional flooding was reported at Dade Avenue and Purdy Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of higher than normal tidal departures coupled with the October full moon spring tide resulted in periodic minor flooding of low lying and vulnerable areas at high tides for several days during the end of the month. Several roads, including major arteries, along the Palm Beach, Broward, and Miami-Dade coast experienced saw flooding high enough to result in road closures during times of high tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High tide flooding in Matheson Hammock Park resulted in ankle deep water in the parking lot, beach, and picnic areas as well as swamped a normally separated man-made lagoon. Water reached up to the local beach front restaurant but did not enter the building. Flood waters receded after 1030am. Information from ABC Local 10. Flooding also occurred again on October 26 and 27th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent onshore flow combined with elevated Atlantic water levels due to much higher than normal astronomical tides with the approach and passage of the Super Harvest Blood Moon (occurred on Sept. 27) funneled a lot of water into the St. Johns River basin. Elevated water levels, some the highest experienced since the 2004 tropical storms (Jeanne and Frances) caused flooding of low lying areas and some property. One of the hardest hit areas were near Dunns Creek, Welaka and Satsuma in Putnam county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant river flooding impacted residences along the St Johns River and Dunns Creek area. Some residences along Dunns Creek at water flood into their homes over 16 days, with the highest values at high tide. Some river front homes in the Sportsman Harbor subdivision had water rushing into their yards, over docks and over other outdoor property. This area is a known area at high risk for flooding (Cat 1 storm surge inundation zone and flood zone), and has repetitive losses due to river flood impacts. Putnam County EM reported that most flood impacts were considered minor during this high water period. ||In a report, Putnam County Emergency Management observed minor flooding in residential areas of the St. Johns River and Dunn���s Creek since October 5, 2015. No flooding affected habitability was reported to Putnam County Emergency Management. In accordance with Florida Division of Emergency Management and FEMA damage assessment guidance, all residential impacts were categorized as ���minimally affected.��� Most observed impacts were standing water in roadways, private driveways and public drainage systems.

Areas that were impacted were along Cedar Road along the SW bank of Doctor's Lake and Deer Island Road along the St. Johns River south of the Bayard Conservation Area near the mouth of Clarkes Creek.  In both areas, tidal flooding periodically closed road access to homes and properties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A full moon on Oct. 27th produced higher than normal astronomical tides and combined with moderate onshore flow created minor coastal flooding and beach erosion around the times of high tide.  Water level departures along the St. Johns River basin were up to 1.5 ft above predicted levels and up to 0.75 ft above predictions along the Atlantic coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 9:12 am local time, the tide gage at Mayport measured a water level of 3.91 ft above NAVD88 datum. Minor coastal flooding typically begins at 3.8 ft. At 11 am local time, the tide gage at the Main Street Bridge in downtown Jacksonville measured a water level of 2.13 ft above NAVD88 datum. Minor coastal flooding typically begins at 2.0 ft.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tidal flooding resulted in closure of Indian Creek Drive (A1A) between 27th Street and 41st Street with the morning high tide.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High surf and strong winds resulted in significant coastal flooding in the Ft. Pickens area. Sands drifts as high as 5 feet were left in the wake of the flood waters on portions of Ft. Pickens Rd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 9 year old boy at St. Pete Beach was pushed out to sea by strong rip currents. His remains were found by local police two days later. A rip current statement was in effect at the time.

Modest instability and shear was in place with supported scattered severe storms. A waterspout was observed on Sante Fe Lake in Alachua County, but dissipated before making landfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant wind damage to trees with numerous power lines down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered afternoon thunderstorms developed in an environment favorable for severe wet microbursts with high Wet Microburst Severity Index values.  In addition, a brief tornado touched down near Indian Pass in Gulf county.  This was a rare summer tornado not caused by a tropical cyclone.  It is likely that there was some locally high shear values in places due to a boundary interaction with the sea breeze.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down and blocked Middle Creek Road.

A weak surface trough was over south Georgia, and this feature combined with the sea breezes over an unstable and moist airmass produced scattered severe storms during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning caused a structure fire at Wayne Frier Mobile Home Sales at 12788 US Highway 90 West in Live Oak.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active pattern as a mid level trough rotated over the region with a couple of embedded short wave troughs bringing a couple rounds of strong to severe storms across the local area. Best instability was over northeast Florida where more heating occurred prior to storm arrival under SW flow. Strong shear was present which favored wind gusts in storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down across the road at 21412 51st Drive.

Best instability was over northeast Florida where more heating occurred prior to storm arrival under SW flow. Strong shear was present which favored wind gusts in storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level trough moving across North Florida led to scattered showers and storms across South Florida and local Atlantic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck house on the 3500 block of SW 123rd Court leading to an attic fire that spread across entire roof. Information via public and broadcast media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak but slow moving area of low pressure combined with tropical moisture in place to produce excessive rainfall across portions of Taylor and Dixie counties.  The Steinhatchee area was particularly hard hit with a combination of flash flooding, areal flooding, and river flooding.  Over 10 of rainfall was measured in the Cross City area of Dixie county in just a couple of days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding occurred with intense rainfall rates. Some cars were submerged, water was up to a few feet deep over roadways including Highway 358, and water affected some homes. Some pictures were posted on social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak but slow moving area of low pressure combined with tropical moisture in place to produce excessive rainfall across portions of Taylor and Dixie counties.  The Steinhatchee area was particularly hard hit with a combination of flash flooding, areal flooding, and river flooding.  Over 10 of rainfall was measured in the Cross City area of Dixie county in just a couple of days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding transitioned to a long duration of areal flooding across southeastern Taylor county. Water was slow to recede due to around 10 inches of rain falling in a couple of days. Water affected numerous roadways and homes in the area. Damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist and unstable southerly flow on the south side of a weak low moving across Northern Florida led to numerous afternoon showers and storms across South Florida, especially across Palm Beach County. Several funnel clouds and two tornadoes were reported along with straight line wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EF-0 tornado touched down touched down along lake in Village Walk Subdivision then moved northeast, crossing Forest Hill Blvd and the Florida Turnpike before moving into Okeeheelee Park and dissipating. Video showed touchdown in Village Walk with no known damage and discontinuous damage path across roads into park. Most of damage from tornado occurred in park with downed trees and large branches down as well as damaged soccer goals, a judge's stand, and a blown power transformer. A roof was also blown off in the park. Reports and video received via broadcast media and public.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level trough moving across North Florida led to scattered showers and storms across South Florida and local Atlantic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person killed and 2 injured by lightning strike near a group of men sheltering under tree in park in Northeast Miami-Dade County. Exact location and time estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist and unstable atmosphere led to scattered showers and storms across South Florida. A severe storm produced straight line wind damage across Clewiston in Hendry County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hendry County Sheriff's Office reported minor damage across portions of Clewiston. Damage occurred to a residential shed and fence along with several uprooted trees and a trampoline thrown against a house near Georgia Ave and 7th Street. Additional uprooted trees led to minor roof damage and fence at Clewiston Middle School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous afternoon sea breeze thunderstorms developed along the I-75 corridor during the late afternoon hours. A few storms reached severe levels producing hail and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported quarter size hail falling near Bronson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level trough over the eastern Gulf of Mexico and surface boundary across South Florida lead to showers and storms over South Florida during the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Naples Airport (KAPF) observer reported funnel cloud near Gordon Pass. The funnel was visible for 5 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Erika moved across South Florida, leading to widespread showers and a few storms. Minor flooding was reported in associated with this activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Off-duty NWS employee reported several impassable streets due to flooding in the Wynwood area of Miami from remnant low pressure of Tropical Storm Erika.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered convection developed across the tri-state area with isolated strong to severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Spears Road as well as limbs down on the road near Highway 2 and Highway 177.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of slow moving thunderstorms sat over portions of Washington and Jackson county during the evening hours of April 12th and lingered into the overnight hours.  Significant flash flooding resulted in Jackson county with up to 11 inches of rain falling in a 6-8 hour period.  This resulted in numerous road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scott Church Road was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the area with an unusually strong low-level jet.  In fact, this was a record breaking event in terms of upper air. The 62 knot wind measured by the TAE balloon at 850 mb ties the record for the strongest 850 mb wind measured by a Tallahassee balloon for either a 00z or 12z launch. Although an inversion was present, the airmass modified quickly, and the southeast big bend (usually a severe weather report minimum in the area) had a lot of trees and power lines down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in the Cross City area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain associated with the intersection of a stalled frontal boundary and a coastal trough along the Treasure Coast trained into the coasts of St. Lucie and Martin Counties during the morning and early afternoon hours. Additional slow moving storms developed in the afternoon producing heavy rain and small hail in Central Okeechobee County. ||Twenty-four hour rainfall totals reached 8-11 inches in and around the Palm City and Stuart areas with 3-7 inch amounts around Port St. Lucie. Radar estimated rainfall totals were up to 7 inches across Central Okeechobee County. The majority of this rainfall occurred in a period of 6-hours or less. ||Flooding of many roadways occurred, stranding many vehicles. Water entered several homes in Palm City. Drainage canals overflowed and standing water reached a foot in many locations and was slow to recede. Standing water was reported on roadways in Central Okeechobee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A SKYWARN spotter in The Vikings (also known at The Prairie) subdivision reported 10 to 15 minutes of small hail up to nickel sized associated with a strong storm moving over the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm formed along the sea breeze and a southward shifting frontal boundary across Flagler county. A passing upper level short wave trough combined with the surface convergence and diurnal instability over Flagler which resulted in some hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime size hail was reported near Eagle Rock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front combined with passing upper level short wave trough energy fueled scattered pre-frontal storms. Cool upper level temperatures combined with diurnal instability fueld a lone severe storm over Columbia county that produced large hail.

A line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across the region on April 19th, bringing with it tornadoes and straight line wind damage.  Then on April 20th, another line of severe storms moved from Gulf county eastward through Tallahassee, bringing down numerous trees and power lines along its path, especially in Tallahassee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 8 reports of trees and power lines down were received, mainly in the northern areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced lightning which caused damage in northwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike resulted in a house fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light steering flow was in place which enabled both west and east coast sea breezes to penetrate inland through the day. Sea breeze forcing interacted with high moisture and marginal instability to fuel scattered pulse severe storms which produced wind damage and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down in Flagler Estates. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered summertime convection developed during the afternoon hours with a few storms becoming severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto Cherry Tree Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong seabreeze induced thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon and persisted into the evening hours. Most stayed below severe thresholds but still produced some isolated gusty winds leading to some minor damage. One storm produced a lightning strike that hit a home in Pinellas County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A resident near New Tampa reported some damage to shingles and outdoor patio furniture. Based off radar data damage was likely due to a sub-severe downburst winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered afternoon thunderstorms developed in an environment favorable for severe wet microbursts with high Wet Microburst Severity Index values.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree that was 2 feet in diameter fell on Veterans Memorial Drive near Moccasin Gap and blocked the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered afternoon thunderstorms developed in an environment favorable for severe wet microbursts with high Wet Microburst Severity Index values.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near CR365 and SR267 partially blocking the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak area of surface low pressure continued just north of the local area with a prevailing WSW steering flow focusing the highest afternoon and evening rain chances along the St. Johns River basin. An unstable low level airmass fueled a few strong to severe storms, one which popped across Putnam county causing extensive wind damage off of Browns Fish Camp Road near Crescent City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down along State Road 16 near the intersection of County Road 16-A. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: South to southwest steering flow focused the sea breeze merger toward the I-95 corridor in the late afternoon and early evening where several downbursts occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fallen tree blocked the intersection of Lasalle Street and Palm Avenue in San Marco. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak mid level low pressure system meandered over north Florida with a moist SW flow in place. Slow moving storms produced heavy rainfall and gusty winds generally below 50 mph.

A surface front meandered over N Florida with a dominant west coast sea breeze regime. High moisture from the Gulf and an unstable airmass fueled scattered severe storms that broke out across NE Florida, while a remnant bow echo raced across S Georgia and triggered additional storms across SE Ga during the mid afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down over County Road 249 and 40th Street. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southerly steering flow prevailed with a surface ridge offshore of the Carolina coast. Active sea breezes over an unstable air mass combined with outflows produced scattered severe storms with gusty winds the main hazard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down by Buleah Church. Cost of damage was estimated for the event to be included in Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak but slow moving area of low pressure combined with tropical moisture in place to produce excessive rainfall across portions of Taylor and Dixie counties.  The Steinhatchee area was particularly hard hit with a combination of flash flooding, areal flooding, and river flooding.  Over 10 of rainfall was measured in the Cross City area of Dixie county in just a couple of days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding transitioned to a long duration of areal flooding across southeastern Taylor county.  Water was slow to recede due to around 10 inches of rain falling in a couple of days.  Water affected numerous roadways and homes in the area.  The emergency manager estimated property damage at $173,500.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually strong upper level winds for summer combined with high instability and steep mid level lapse rates to produce numerous reports of severe weather across the tri-state area.  Multiple rounds of severe weather occurred on June 30th, especially across the Florida Panhandle.  The city of Tallahassee reported that 160 tons of yard waste was generated from the severe weather, requiring extra work time to remove storm debris.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on River Road south of I-10 with power lines also down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A transformer was blown near the intersection of Apalachee Pkwy and Blairstone.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst associated with a severe thunderstorm produced a 97 mph wind gust from the southwest. The extreme winds were recorded by USAF wind tower 0506, near the NASA Headquarters building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: South to southwest steering flow focused the sea breeze merger toward the I-95 corridor in the late afternoon and early evening where several downbursts occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees were blown down.

A weak surface trough was over south Georgia, and this feature combined with the sea breezes over an unstable and moist airmass produced scattered severe storms during the afternoon and evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough across SE Georgia which combined with a moist and unstable airmass triggered strong to severe storms across the local area during the afternoon and evening hours. Activity started along the Gulf Coast in the morning, then tracked eastward through the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of Cassat Avenue at I-10 was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across the region on April 19th, bringing with it tornadoes and straight line wind damage.  Then on April 20th, another line of severe storms moved from Gulf county eastward through Tallahassee, bringing down numerous trees and power lines along its path, especially in Tallahassee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto a house on Okaloosa Street.  Damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved through the Florida peninsula ahead of a cold front early on the morning of the 24th. One of these storms caused a short lived tornado in southern Sarasota County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Myakka State Forest Ranger reported tornado damage to a ranger station and a mobile home trailer at around 3:50 am.  A subsequent NWS storm survey found a 50 yard wide, one mile long intermittent path of damage that included snapped and uprooted trees and a mobile home trailer that had been picked up and tossed 30 to 40 yards. The damage was found to be consistent with an EF-0 tornado making multiple touchdowns with estimated peak winds of 85 mph. The survey found low end EF-0 damage to the east of the Myakka River that included minor roof damage, a bent stop sign pole, and numerous broken tree branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure over the eastern U.S. caused numerous afternoon showers and thunderstorms over the eastern half of the peninsula. Sporadic tree damage was noted in an area from Doral to Florida International University campus, then east to Fountainbleu. A total of 12,940 customers reported power outages in Miami-Dade County, with an additional 3,520 in Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime sized hail was reported at SW 117th Ct. and 32nd Terrace.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms produced hundreds of lightning strikes across the Tampa Bay Area during the late evening on Sunday. One lightning strike hit a house in Land O' Lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A newspaper reported that a lightning strike caused a fire to a home in Hernando. The residents were not home, and fire crews quickly put out the fire, however, the newspaper reported that damage was estimated at $172,750.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered summertime convection developed during the afternoon hours with a few storms becoming severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A WCTV viewer reported about 10 trees down just north of Monticello.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along the east coast sea breeze as it pushed inland and a few become strong to severe as they approached the Interstate 4 corridor. Small hail was reported near Ocoee, and up to quarter size to the east of Sanford.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported hail up to the size of quarters near Midway, east of Sanford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered afternoon thunderstorms developed in an environment favorable for severe wet microbursts with high Wet Microburst Severity Index values.  In addition, a brief tornado touched down near Indian Pass in Gulf county.  This was a rare summer tornado not caused by a tropical cyclone.  It is likely that there was some locally high shear values in places due to a boundary interaction with the sea breeze.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Florida Highway Patrol reported a tree in the road near the intersection of Natural Bridge Road and Woodville Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The continuing combination of higher than normal tidal departures coupled with the perigean spring tide on September 29th resulted in periodic minor flooding of low lying and vulnerable areas at high tides for several days during the end of September. Several roads, including major arteries, along the Palm Beach, Broward, and Miami-Dade coast experienced saw flooding high enough to result in road closures during times of high tide. Additional minor road flooding occurred along roads in Naples.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water inundating parking lots along 71st Street in Normandy Isles due to tidal flooding. Report received from broadcast media via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level low and associated surface low caused several days of heavy rains along the Gulf Coast. Ahead of this system, a large amounts of gulf moisture was pumped into the region. This lead to flash flooding and also some severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding on Highway 98, Daniel Drive and Nightingdale Drive in Gulf Breeze.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level low and associated surface low caused several days of heavy rains along the Gulf Coast. Ahead of this system, a large amounts of gulf moisture was pumped into the region. This lead to flash flooding and also some severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding occurred in the Destin area due to slow moving thunderstorms. Destin Airport recorded a storm total of 12.54 inches. A trained spotter reported several feet of water due to the heavy rain in multiple locations near Destin. Some of the roads impacted include FL Hwy 293, US Highway 98, and around Middle Bay Bridge. Numerous cars were stalled due to the flood waters. The flood waters made it into the garages of a few homes in the Quail Run subdivision on Airport Rd.

Heavy rains with these storms aggravated ongoing tidal flooding leading to minor flooding across portions of Naples.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several videos posted on social media show waterspout that developed near the coast over the water moved onto the beach between the Ritz-Carlton and Remington at Bay Colony hotels. Tornado briefly reached into the dune line where it picked up branches and tree debris before dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of T.S. Erika crossed the forecast area from the Gulf region through the day. High moisture combined with forcing and weak onshore convergence around the remnant low brought waves of heavy rainfall and isolated strong storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck Sally B. Mathis Elementary School. School was evacuated due to smoke in the building from a damaged air conditioner unit. The cost of damage was unknown but estimated for StormData.

A southwest Palm Coast spotter measured 1.30 within 40 minutes. In Eagle Rock a spotter measured 1.90 within 40 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure across the NE Gulf of Mexico drifted N/NW with waves of rainfall moving across the local area due to high moisture content and forcing around the low.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant road flooding was reported at the intersection of Old Moultrie Road and US Highway 1. Water depth was up to a few feet with some stranded cars.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty employee measured 3.07 of rainfall in an hour and a half.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A social media post indicated a tree was blown down near the intersection of Fort Caroline Road and McCormick Road. The time of damage was based on radar and the cost of damage was known but estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported in downtown St. Augustine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper trough of low pressure over the eastern U.S. caused scattered afternoon thunderstorms across South Florida. A few strong storms produced small hail in eastern Palm Beach county, as well as a funnel cloud over Broward county. Lightning strikes  started fires in two Miami-Dade County homes and resulted in about 3,700 customers losing power in Aventura, including at Aventura Mall. Traffic lights were out at several intersections along Biscayne Boulevard in northeast Miami-Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reported near the intersection of I-595 and The Florida Turnpike.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A suspected lightning strike lead to a fire at an apartment in the Playa Lago Condominiums in the Tamiami neighborhood of Miami. Neighbors reported hearing loud thunder, then seeing smoke and flames coming from the affected apartment. Fire damage was confined to one apartment, but several others received water damage. No one was home at the time of the fire and no injures were reported. Information from NBC6. Time of event estimated and based on data from lightning detection network.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Daily total rainfall of 3.10 inches was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving frontal boundary shifted inland across NE Florida with a surface low starting to form offshore of the local Atlantic coast. Onshore flow and high low level moisture content brought waves of locally heavy rainfall in convergent bands along the NE Florida Atlantic coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy rainfall in convergent bands brought heavy rainfall to areas of Flagler county. A bridge about 4 miles SW of Espanola along County Road 302 measured a daily rainfall of 4 inches with some minor washouts on dirt roads in the area. Other rainfall measurements across the county ranged from 1.70 at the Flagler EOC, 2.50 in Mondex and 2 in St. Johns Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Waves of heavy rainfall moving inland within convergent bands caused flooding in some areas of NE Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of elevated water levels and recent heavy rainfall cause street flooding along the waterfront of historic St. Augustine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep and moist southerly wind flow led to an active afternoon of thunderstorms across parts of southeast Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a garage roof of a home in Jupiter Landings. Palm Beach County Fire Rescue and Florida Power & Light officials responded and found a damaged rafter under the shingles, a charred electrical line and blown circuit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper trough of low pressure over the eastern U.S. caused scattered afternoon thunderstorms across South Florida. A few strong storms produced small hail in eastern Palm Beach county, as well as a funnel cloud over Broward county. Lightning strikes  started fires in two Miami-Dade County homes and resulted in about 3,700 customers losing power in Aventura, including at Aventura Mall. Traffic lights were out at several intersections along Biscayne Boulevard in northeast Miami-Dade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SUV traveling along I-95 near Griffin Road exit was disabled by lightning strike on rear of vehicle. A hole the size of a quarter was found in the rear of the vehicle beneath the back windshield and the electrical system in the car was destroyed. No injuries were reported and vehicle was towed from scene.

Reports of numerous cars stalled across the area were received via social media and broadcast media, with most of the reports coming from the Kings Creek community north of Kendall Drive and west of the Palmetto Expressway. Miami Doppler radar estimated nearly four inches of rain in a short amount of time had occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The local area was within the right-rear quadrant of a lifting upper level jet which brought fairly deep layer lift and a rise in moisture levels from the Gulf region. A marginal risk of severe storms was highlighted for the area given strong forcing and moisture content.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Apparent Rear Flank downdraft contacted ground. damage for about 1 mile downstream with a 100 yard wide swath. most debris laid out in straight line path with some lighter porch materials blown to the south. Likely the lighter materials were caught in an eddy. Trees and power lines downed, some roof damage with an open shed-garage area blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level trough of low pressure over southern Alabama along with a tropical wave across the central Gulf of Mexico placed South Florida in deep tropical moisture with scattered late afternoon and early evening thunderstorms over the mainland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado initially touched down in Imagination Farms community north of Griffin Road and west of Flamingo Road in Davie. The tornado traveled W-SW over Imaginary Farms and uprooted several medium to large trees as well as breaking off large branches. One home had about a dozen roof tiles removed/shifted in a few places in Imaginary Farms, with most of the damage oriented in a NE-SW direction. Damage became less noticeable at Griffin Road, with only minor damage to branches.||The tornado appeared to have lifted briefly just south of Griffin Road as it entered Southwest Rances, with very little damage. Therefore, this tornado likely had a discontinuous path.||Damage increased again west of SW 148th Avenue in the Ivanhoe Estates neighborhood of Davie between Griffin and Sterling Roads as it continued to move SW. A second area of peak intensity and evidence of rotation along Castlegate Avenue north of Waterford Drive. A large black olive tree about 3 feet in diameter at the base uprooted in a southwest direction with nearby large tree branches and debris oriented in an eastward direction. One home had about a dozen tiles removed or shifted. The tornado apparently lifted just east of I-75 in Ivanhoe Estates.||In total, at least two dozen trees were uprooted or had medium to large sized branches snapped. At least two homes sustained minor roof damage. Most damage rated on low end of EF0 scale near 65 mph, with only two or three spots of peak intensity of 70 to 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure near the Savannah River trailed a coastal trough offshore of the local coastline. A large upper level low was over NW Georgia. A few heavy rainfall producing showers and storms developed across NE Florida.  Elevated waters levels along the coast were near 0.75 ft above predictions which caused some minor coastal flooding around the times of high tide.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding due to heavy rainfall impacted Cedar Point Road and Pumpkin Hill Road. There was about 4-8 inches of water over the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of persistent and the approach of the full Super Moon of 2015 created higher than normal tides along the local coast with minor flooding impacts around the times of high tides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High astronomical tides were recorded at the NOS station at Mayport. At 8 pm local time on 9/26, the gage measured 3.77 ft NAVD88 datum. This was about 0.42 above the highest astronomical tide of 3.35 ft above NAVD88 datum. . At 9 pm the NOS tidal gage on the St. Johns River at the Dames Point Bridge reached 3.21 ft which was about 0.80 ft above the highest astronomical tide of 2.41 ft above NAVD88 datum. At 10 pm, the public reported via social media street flooding near Hogan's Creek in downtown Jacksonville on East Monroe Street due to high tide along the creek. At 8:24 am on 9/27 the NOS Station at Mayport reached 3.79 ft which was about 0.44 ft above the highest astronomical tide of 3.35 ft above NAVD88 datum. At 9:36 am the NOS tide gage on the St. Johns River at the Dames Point Bridge reached 3.23 ft which was about 0.82 ft above the highest astronomical tide of 2.41 ft above NAVD88. At 11 am on 9/28, Clarks Fish Camp in the Julington Creek had tidal flooding in the parking lot and inside the restaurant for the past 3-4 days. At 11 am on 9/30, significant beach erosion of 2-4 ft occurred along north portions of Atlantic Beach over the past few days. Tidal marks showed water reached the dune line at most locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of persistent and the approach of the full Super Moon of 2015 created higher than normal tides along the local coast with minor flooding impacts around the times of high tides.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On 9/26 at 8:45 pm the NOS tide gage in Fernandina Beach reported 4.96 ft above NAVD88 which was 0.58 ft above the highest astronomical tide of 4.38 ft NAVD88. At 8:54 am on 9/27, the NOS gage at Fernandina Beach reach 4.93 ft which was 0.55 ft above the highest astronomical tide of 4.38 ft NAVD88 datum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of higher than normal tidal departures coupled with the perigean spring tide resulted in periodic minor flooding of low lying and vulnerable areas at high tides for several days during the end of September. Several roads, including major arteries, along the Palm Beach, Broward, and Miami-Dade coast experienced saw flooding high enough to result in road closures during times of high tide. Additional minor road flooding occurred along roads in Naples.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Miami Beach PD reported the right lane of Collins Avenue in Miami Beach was flooded and impassable between 15th and 24th streets in Miami Beach. Additional reports via social media also reported flooding on Jefferson Avenue north of Lincoln Avenue with the morning high tide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning associated with a strong thunderstorm struck a home in Winter Garden and caused a fire that nearly destroyed the home. A short time later, another thunderstorm became locally strong over the Kissimmee Airport, with microbust winds damaging a hanger building.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Osceola County Emergency Management surveyed damage at the Kissimmee Airport due to a thunderstorm. Downburst winds estimated at 50 mph produced damage to a single metal hanger building (41 x 51 feet). The building door, facing north, was open at the time of the strong winds. The nearby ASOS recorded north wind gusts to 39 mph. No other damage noted within the vicinity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving weather system developed over the eastern Gulf of Mexico and eventually crossed the Florida peninsula. This system, combined with unusually deep-layer moisture (even for September) and onshore winds to produce several days of heavy rainfall. Three day rainfall totals reached 6-12 inches in parts of Indian River, St. Lucie and Martin Counties, especially across the barrier islands and easternmost portions of the mainland peninsula. Temporary, minor flooding of many roadways, parking lots and yards resulted as retention ponds and drainage canals overflowed. Rainfall totals of 3-6 inches, locally higher impacted Brevard and Volusia Counties, but localized flooding in these areas occurred only in poor drainage areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multi-day rainfall totals reached 6-12 inches across much of eastern St. Lucie County. The highest measured rain gauge totals were 12.25 inches at the Nettles Island COOP site and 11.19 inches at Savannah Preserve. Temporary flooding of roadways, parking lots and yards occurred as retention ponds and drainage canals overflowed their banks. County Road 707 from Walton Road to the Martin County line was closed for several days due to washouts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level trough of low pressure over southern Alabama along with a tropical wave across the central Gulf of Mexico placed South Florida in deep tropical moisture with scattered late afternoon and early evening thunderstorms over the mainland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off-duty NWS employee took video of a funnel cloud over Pembroke Pines. Location was estimated on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor coastal flooding occurred in a few Florida Bayside neighborhoods in North Key Largo, due mostly to persistent large northeast fetch occurring offshore the southeast U.S. Coast.  Due to the very small diurnal tidal range of 2 to 3 inches in the far eastern end of Florida Bay and Blackwater Sound, flooding of the lowest coastal streets was continuous after it began on Sep. 21.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Continuous coastal flooding of streets began approximately 17:00 EST on September  21st in the area of Adams and Shaw Drive in North Key Largo's bayside, gradually expanding to adjacent streets throughout the Blackwater Sound and Sexton Cove shorelines.  While no homes or businesses were flooded, the depth of water reached as high as 0.8 feet by September 30th, with a couple streets becoming impassable to small vehicles.  The flooding was confirmed by Monroe County Emergency Management and Key Largo Fire Rescue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A classic heavy rainfall scenario set up along the NE Florida Atlantic Coast where a stationary surface front meandered in the lower levels with strong onshore flow on the northern side of the boundary. Aloft deep layer SW flow and strong upper level divergence within the right-rear quadrant of a strong upper level jet enhanced deep layer lift over the stationary front. Strong low level convergence, high moisture, and the upper level support produced prolonged heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter measured 8.4 inches of daily rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A classic heavy rainfall scenario set up along the NE Florida Atlantic Coast where a stationary surface front meandered in the lower levels with strong onshore flow on the northern side of the boundary. Aloft deep layer SW flow and strong upper level divergence within the right-rear quadrant of a strong upper level jet enhanced deep layer lift over the stationary front. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread and significant flash flooding impacts occurred from Mayport southward to Neptune Beach during the evening hours with over 10 inches of rainfall measured at Naval Station Mayport for the day, but of which fell within 6 hours. The first report of flooding was reported around 7:26 pm by Fire Dept/Rescue with a road flooded just south of the Wonderwood Expressway. By 8:15 pm, the county emergency management  (EM) reported water flooded homes and apartments off of Mayport Road near the 2100 block. Around 9 pm, EM received multiple reports of roads flooded between 1st and 3rd Streets in Jacksonville Beach. EM also reported apartments were flooded between 2130 and 2160 Mayport Road, roads were flooded in Mayport Village along Broad Street and Ribault Street, numerous streets were closed in Neptune Beach due to flooding, and several residences along Forest Avenue, Oak Street and Magnolia Street has water within them. A vehicle drove into the ditch along the 100 block of Penman Road because the driver could not make out the edge of the road due to flooding. Also around 9 pm, EM relayed that flooding impacted all of Atlantic Beach with the worst hit areas including Aquatic Drive, Cavella Road, Skate Road, Selva Marina Drive and the north end of the city in the area of 19th Street and 20th Street. Many of these roads were completely inundated with water and water flowed into some residences along these streets. The police department responded to several calls for stranded vehicles due to flood water. At 9 pm, EM reported a few units at the Admirals Walk Apartment complex were affected by flood waters. Units at Mariners Village Apartments were also flooded. The Arium Apartments at 2160 Mayport Road at 32 units affected by flooding last night. The Red Cross set up a service center to assist affected residents. Six condos along Seminole Road had water flow inside. Around 10:12 pm, a spotter reported water across the Days Inn parking lot near Atlantic and Aquatic drive with about 3 ft of standing water in the lot. Cars were submerged in the parking lot. Water entered part of the hotel. Aquatic drive was closed due to flood water about 2 ft deep in some areas. At 10:41 am on Nov. 9th, EM reported Palm Avenue in Atlantic Beach was impassable due to flooding. The street runs into Howell Park which was under water. The creek near city hall along Seminole Road and Palm Avenue is still very high but has subsided some and the parking lot for city hall and Russell Park is not longer flooded. The cost of damage was unknown, and is likely an underestimate.

Aloft deep layer SW flow and strong upper level divergence within the right-rear quadrant of a strong upper level jet enhanced deep layer lift over the stationary front. Strong low level convergence, high moisture, and the upper level support produced prolonged heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS JAX storm survey determined that flash flooding occurred during the evening and overnight hours within the McCoy's Creek basin on Sunday Nov. 8th as runoff from heavy rainfall was backed up by the evening high tide. On Monday Nov. 9th, flooding occurred during the morning hours within the basin. The primary flooding source east of Stockton Street was due to tidal departures of 1-1.5 ft above normal tides. West of Stockton Street fresh water rainfall runoff was expected to be the primary cause of flooding. Kings Street was also flooded. Parts of McCoys Creek Blvd were closed due to flood waters.  Cost of damage was unknown but estimated for Storm Data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably deep moisture moved into the area and combined with a weak area of low pressure to produce excessive rainfall across portions of the area.  At Apalachicola, 3.77 inches of rain was measured in just one hour.  Storm total rainfall over a 2 day period ranged from 9 to 11 inches across portions of Franklin county extended westward to Bay county.  In Holmes county, preliminary road damage from flooding was estimated at around $500,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water entered the first floor of some houses on Vecuna Circle.  Nearby, a cocorahs site measured a storm total of 11.25 inches, most of which fell within a 2 day period.  Damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A classic heavy rainfall scenario set up along the NE Florida Atlantic Coast where a stationary surface front meandered in the lower levels with strong onshore flow on the northern side of the boundary. Aloft deep layer SW flow and strong upper level divergence within the right-rear quadrant of a strong upper level jet enhanced deep layer lift over the stationary front. Strong low level convergence, high moisture, and the upper level support produced prolonged heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least 7 inches of daily rainfall was measured since midnight in East Arlington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough and surface cold front affected the region during November 18th.  Three tornadoes were confirmed in northwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado with winds estimated around 70 mph touched down in Gadsden county near Gretna and tracked northeast across Hardaway Highway.  One tree was blown down onto Hardaway Highway and a dual-pol tornadic debris signature was observed from KTLH radar at that location.  This is a rural area with no structures impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough and surface cold front affected the region during November 18th.  Three tornadoes were confirmed in northwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touched down on Eglin Air Force Base property in western Walton county.  There are no structures in this area to impact, but a brief dual-pol tornadic debris signature was observed on KEVX radar with the storm.  A soil conservation technician from Eglin AFB was dispatched to investigate the area for any signs of a tornado.  The technician found green twigs of swampbay magnolia (Magnolia Virginiana) that were displaced 200 yards downstream from where they grow at the exact coordinates of the tornadic debris signature seen on dual-pol radar.  The tornado was rated EF0.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level trough and surface cold front affected the region during November 18th.  Three tornadoes were confirmed in northwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported sighting a brief tornado touchdown near Highway 331 north of the Eglin AFB territory in western Walton county.  The tornado did not produce any structural damage.  This was the same thunderstorm that produced the brief tornado and dual-pol tornadic debris signature while over the Eglin AFB territory.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty showers developed and moved through the Florida peninsula during the afternoon and evening hours. Some of these gusts did minor damage around Avon Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Florida Department of Emergency Management relayed a report of a secondary roof pulled off of an older mobile home that downed a power line in Avon Park, along with a trampoline flipped over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into the tri-state area with heavy rainfall and some severe weather.  In northwest Florida, an EF1 tornado touched down in Washington county with other straight line wind damage in Jackson county.  In addition, some flooding occurred in Walton county with over 6 inches of rain measured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down around Deltona Blvd and Peak Drive, downing a tree and a power line.  It tracked northeast to near Washington Blvd and Syfrett Road where the most significant damage occurred.  At this location, a tree was uprooted, a portion of a metal roof on a barn was torn off, a garage door was ripped off and caused a hole in the roof of a house, and a greenhouse was destroyed.  The tornado continued moving northeast, downing a tree on Highview Circle and quickly lifted thereafter.  The tornado was rated EF1 with max winds around 90 mph.  It also exhibited a tornadic debris signature on dual-pol radar.  Damage cost was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist atmosphere and nearby stationary boundary lead to the development of a line of strong storms that moved east-southeast through South Florida. A funnel cloud was spotted with storms along this line as they moved across southwest Broward County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported a funnel cloud SW of the intersection of 30th Ave and South University Drive. Additional pictures from social media in the vicinity showed also showed a wall cloud on the leading edge of gust front.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into South Florida during the day of December 3rd, and stalled across the far southern end of the peninsula and upper Florida Keys on the 4th and 5th. Several rounds of heavy rainfall fell across South Florida during the three day period, with the hardest hit areas across Southern Miami-Dade county. Rainfall amounts near 15 fell across Homestead, the Redlands, and western Kendall, with 4-8 reported across the remainder of Miami-Dade county and into coastal Broward county, most of which occurred during the day of December 5th. This rainfall led to significant flooding in Miami-Dade county with numerous road closures and cars stalling in flood waters. Significant damage was done to the fall and winter crops in Miami-Dade county as well as leading to multi-day closures at Zoo Miami due to flooding in the facility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain throughout the day lead to numerous flooded roads and road closures in Eastern Miami-Dade county. Road closures occurred in Aventura at NE 191st street from Biscayne Blvd (US1), and in El Portal, Little Haiti, and Miami-Beach. 1-2ft of standing water occurred in some places along the flooded roadways at times, lingering into the late evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level disturbance combined with unseasonably high moisture and instability resulted in a few severe thunderstorms and one brief tornado in the western Florida Panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service survey team confirmed damage of an EF-1 tornado a little over 2 miles southwest of Chumuckla. The tornado tracked alomst a mile north-northeast before weakening to an EF-0 and dissipating. The team was unable to survey a heavily wooded area to the south. It is possible the track could extend further southward. The tornado tracked through a forested area and across an open field. Damage was generally confined to uprooted pecan trees and well as snapped cedar and pine trees. This damage is consistent with EF-1 wind speeds around 107 mph. The maximum width of the tornado was 75 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The frontal boundary that brought the heavy rainfall earlier in the month briefly moved southwards before shifting back to the north across Miami-Dade county on December 8th. Another period of heavy rain on already saturated ground brought additional flooding to Miami-Dade county. Additional heavy showers developing on the north side of the front brought minor street flooding to Northeast Palm Beach county on December 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in street flooding in the Coral Gables area, mainly in the vicinity of the University of Miami. Law enforcement reported street flooding along LeJuene Road, resulting in several lanes being blocked off to traffic. Additional reports were received of secondary street flooding up to curb level with 1ft of water reports in spots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The frontal boundary that brought the heavy rainfall earlier in the month briefly moved southwards before shifting back to the north across Miami-Dade county on December 8th. Another period of heavy rain on already saturated ground brought additional flooding to Miami-Dade county. Additional heavy showers developing on the north side of the front brought minor street flooding to Northeast Palm Beach county on December 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding reported along the 300 block of A1A, and along US1 and Alt A1A in Jupiter. Flood waters quickly receded after rain ended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Coastal convergence along the east coast and anomalously high moisture lead to a persistent band of showers along the east coast metro during the afternoon and evening hours. Minor road flooding was reported in South Miami-Dade county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road in Lake of the Meadows down to one lane due to flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of significant street flooding received throughout the Hammocks, Country Walk, the Crossings, Kendall Lakes, and Three Lakes areas of South Miami-Dade county. Road flooding was initially reported during the late morning hours with progressively increasing impacts through the afternoon and evening hours. Many roads became impassable with numerous road closures throughout the area. Many cars stalled out in the flood waters and had to be pushed out by tow trucks throughout the night. Zoo Miami reported numerous walkways and exhibits with standing water, forcing the relocation of zoo animals. Many animal exhibit moats were reported 6-8 feet above normal levels, forcing the closure of the zoo for several days. 9 to 10 inches of rain in 12 hours was measured by unofficial observers in The Hammocks and The Crossings. Miami Executive Airport set a record 24-hour rainfall record with 8.92 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into South Florida during the day of December 3rd, and stalled across the far southern end of the peninsula and upper Florida Keys on the 4th and 5th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multi-day heavy rainfall lead to widespread crop damage in the Southwest Miami-Dade farmlands, especially in the Redland, Florida City and Homestead areas. Farmers reported damage ranging from rotting crops due to continuous rainfall to total loss from completely flooded fields. Yellow squash, zucchini and green beans were the main crops affected along with significant damage to other vegetable crops. Initial damage loss estimates are around 1 million dollars with a 70 to 80% loss in crop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line moved across the Florida panhandle, bringing sporadic reports of trees down and power outages due to strong winds and lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across Bob Sikes Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several road closures in Homestead in the Villages of Homestead and Tennessee Estates subdivisions as a result of flooded roadways. Rainfall amounts were in the 6 to 7 inch range, most of it falling in less than 9 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalous upper level pattern developed across the country with a large upper level trough remaining nearly stationary west of the area for several days. Continuous southwest flow brought unseasonably warm temperatures and high moisture content across the area. Excessive rainfall amounts occurred during the last week of the month with amounts in excess of 15 inches falling across the upper portions of the Pea, Choctawhatchee, and Chattahoochee River basins.  This resulted in significant river flooding downstream across the Chattahoochee, Apalachicola, Chipola, and Choctawhatchee basins in northwest Florida.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large part of a tree was blown down across Laird Road.
